This is a backwards-incompatible change, and changing the type of timeout exception fido threw when calling `result()` caused quite a bit of pain until we found a backwards-compatible solution. Maybe we could do the same here?

1. Define client-specific timeout exceptions like RequestsTimeoutError, FidoTimeoutError, AsyncioTimeoutError
1. Make them inherit from both BravadoTimeoutError and their "native" timeout exception, like fido.exceptions.HTTPTimeoutError, requests.exceptions.ReadTimeout or aiohttp.ServerTimeoutError
1. Do the same thing for connection errors (we have the same issue there)

While it'd be ideal if we could implement this unification only in bravado, I'm perfectly fine with extending the API contract between bravado and the HTTP client to require the client to define their own connection and timeout exceptions that need to be subclasses of bravado ones.

I also think there is value in requiring HTTP clients to behave like that: We can be certain that the bravado library with this change will throw consistent timeout and connection exceptions. We'll just require new fido and bravado-asyncio versions to be used with it. I.e. I probably wouldn't make @reraise_timeout_errors fail gracefully.

Quickly: a lineage is what is elsewhere just called a certificate. We use the term internally to create the illusion of consistence across renewals. There is not really any such thing as renewing a cert, to a CA, only getting a new one that might have related/ the same domains in it. Within Certbot, each lineage is attached to a single renewal configuration file, and its related information is stored in the archive and live directories. Then the links in the live directories point to the updated file in the appropriate archive directory whenever you renew.

If we are going to make this change, and what it seems this might be one of many such "exceptions", it would be prudent to have this folded into _meta_data, for example _meta_data['optional_parameters'], similarly to:

https://github.com/F5Networks/f5-common-python/blob/development/f5/bigip/resource.py#L839

Also if we silently remove the description from **kwargs how would the caller know something went wrong? If the caller does not know that a certain attribute does not exist in the specific version, we should inform him by raising exception with appropriate method, rather than just removing.

The only exception to such rule is here, and this is due to the fact that we send the entire __dict__ (minus __meta_data) to the device when we do update(), and state/session parameters are read only in specific situations, so the caller need not to be notified, unless the caller attempt to set them to an illegal value:

https://github.com/F5Networks/f5-common-python/blob/development/f5/bigip/tm/ltm/node.py#L95
https://github.com/F5Networks/f5-common-python/blob/development/f5/bigip/tm/ltm/pool.py#L131

Say an ideal artman user config is in the following field order

"""
field1:
  field1_a:
  field1_b:
  field_c:

field2:
  field2_a:
  field2_b:
  field_c:
"""

The order is defined as "field1, field1_a, field1_b, field2, field2_a, field2_b, field_c" in a flattend manner (vs nested). Note: there is the limitation to its expression as when `field_c` (field name) is defined inside multiple parent fields, it might not end up working. But this won't be a problem in practice.

DHH users do not **need** to know whether the captions they are reading are the original transcript or a translation, they just need those captions to be the correct ones for the video they are viewing.
   
Bear with me while I recap... The purpose of the caption is to aid/help or enable comprehension of the video content. For that to happen, the captions we present to the user must be:

a) original transcript of that video 
OR 
b) translation of the original transcript in another language which they understand

It seems straightforward enough, but if we do not unequivocally link each caption (weather it's the original transcript or its translation) to its originating video, we may end up in the situation we had in KA Lite all up to release 0.15, where we were serving subtitles in Spanish to users who were viewing videos in that language (**just because they were in the same language**), while those subtitles were, in fact, translations of English video transcripts and therefore almost completely unusable with videos in Spanish (valid for all languages that had so-called "dubbed" videos). 

You may argue that it is an edge case, but it's one we already had to deal with, so let's make sure we prevent it in the future. Again, I am not sure this is actually the right place to do it, and I'm only pressing the point to make sure that we do not use **"in the same language as video"** as the **ONLY** criteria to display captions.

> output are escaped with ``\uXXXX`` sequences, and the result is a ``str``
> instance consisting of ASCII characters only.  If ``ensure_ascii`` is
> ``False``, some chunks written to ``fp`` may be ``unicode`` instances.
> This usually happens because the input contains unicode strings or the
